title: Here’s Just How Disgusting Your Kitchen Sponge Is
gist: A Journal of Food Protection study by Germany's Federal Institute for Risk Assessment shows kitchen sponges let E. coli, Salmonella, and S. aureus colonize and multiply within days even from low starting levels, persist after drying, and spread to surfaces with a light touch.

insights.claim: Kitchen sponges let E. coli, Salmonella, and S. aureus, three foodborne pathogens, gain a foothold and multiply within a few days even when initial bacterial levels were low, per a Journal of Food Protection study.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies a common household food-safety hazard.
insights.locator: Scientists from the German Federal Institute

insights.claim: The bacteria persisted in sponges even after they were dried out for several days, undermining drying as a decontamination method.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Drying alone does not sterilize sponges.

insights.claim: Once colonized, a sponge spreads bacteria to other surfaces with only a light touch, likely contaminating kitchenware that contacts food.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Highlights the cross-contamination vector in kitchens.

top_entities: German Federal Institute for Risk Assessment
